title: Planning a scrapbook double-page spread
subtitle: How to design two facing pages as a single visual unit
excerpt: A double-page spread in a scrapbook is more than two adjacent pages with content. When designed as a unit, the two pages share a visual direction: a dominant photo, a colour palette, and a line of movement that pulls the eye across the gutter. This article covers how to plan and build a spread from a single anchor photograph.

glossaryTerms.definition: The dominant image in a scrapbook spread, the largest photograph, placed first and used to set the colour palette, visual weight, and subject for the other elements on the pages.

A design technique in which three points of visual interest are placed across the spread so the eye moves between them in a triangular path. The technique prevents the eye from settling on a single point and draws attention across the full layout.

content.text: , the single strongest image from the event or moment the spread documents. Everything else on the spread is subordinate to it in size. The focal photo sets the palette: pull two or three colours from it and use those colours for the paper, embellishments, and title text on both pages.

items.name: Photographs, printed
items.notes: The focal photo at its largest printed size plus supporting photos at a smaller consistent size.

items.name: Background paper or card
items.notes: A solid colour or subtle pattern in a tone pulled from the focal photo.

items.name: Pencil
items.notes: For marking positions on the layout before committing with adhesive.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For clean folds on matted photos or layered paper pieces.

items.name: Craft knife and cutting mat
items.notes: For trimming paper mats and cutting embellishment shapes.

items.name: Metal ruler, 30 cm
items.notes: For straight cuts and matting borders.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: For adhering photos and paper elements. Use sparingly under photographs.

content.text: Draw two rectangles side by side representing the two pages. Sketch the position of the focal photo, the supporting photos, the title, and any journalling text. The focal photo should occupy at least one third of the combined spread area. Supporting photos should not be the same size as the focal photo. Leave clear breathing space around the focal photo, crowding it with equal-sized elements reduces its dominance.

to distribute visual interest across the spread. Place a strong colour or pattern element in three positions on the spread that form a rough triangle, one in the upper portion, one in the lower-left, one in the lower-right, or any triangle that spans both pages. This moves the eye across the gutter and uses the right page as actively as the left.

Work from the background outward: lay background paper first, then photo mats, then photos, then embellishments, then title and journalling text last. Each layer is adhered before the next is added. Positioning photographs without adhesive first, and checking the full layout before committing, prevents repositioning damage.
